sentences.text: Yeah.
I was talking about the German lady who was a Baltic German lady who worked for the neighbors in the neighbor factory.
And she was-- and-- and she was extremely kind.
Not only did she bring us food at night, which was absolute no-no to enter a Jewish house.
And she came in the dark, took-- took-- took her life into her hands.
Came in the dark and brought us special goodies and cakes and cookies which she had prepared.
And marinated mushrooms.
I remember so well.
They came in big jars.
And-- and not only that.
We gave her all-- gave that lady all our jewelry.
And would you believe it?
She kept it all to the end of the war.
She found the people-- the owners of the jewelry-- and she send it to each one.
And then she lied down and died, as if she was holding out.
I got my mom's ring, my mom's necklace [POINTING] and all what we gave her.
And she had returned everything, everything, everything.
In those days, we were still living in our own houses.
And the few people who were still left-- I mean my mom, my father wasn't there anymore.
Sela-- [?
Sela ?] Edelstein's husband wasn't there anymore.
[INAUDIBLE] We tried to stick together, like sleeping together in one big room or staying in one bed all together.
We spent a lot of time in bed together.
We all-- people just lying under the blanket feeling cozy and [INAUDIBLE].
And that German lady used to sneak up and bring us-- feed us.
It was an amazing thing to do for her.
Pardon me?
She came constantly.
When the-- when the stuff was finished, she came and took the jars and brought us some-- she got-- she was doing the all the time baking and doing for us.
I-- that's what she did.
No.
We were the family.
We all of us were her family, and she was really devoted.
She was an incredible, brave little lady.
And that she held out to the-- she was much older than us-- held out to the very end of the war.
She was already frail, returned the jewelry, and then only died.
I find it was amazing.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So far, where were we?
Still in our houses, yeah.
And the summer just went by us working and people disappearing by and by, and not knowing what's happening or knowing what's happening.
And then came the winter.
Did you have concerts anymore in that summer?
Oh no, nothing came.
No concerts, nothing.
We then started-- most of us started to work, lots of Jewish girls started to work in the marine clothes depot-- uniforms.
It's where-- was in the [NON-ENGLISH] with-- with-- and it was run by the [?
Marina, ?] which was a German sail-- [? sailors ?] by elderly, elderly officials, officers, [INAUDIBLE].
Their names were Paul von Ribbsdor and Kurt, Kurt-- Otto Hanke, Kurt Dietmar.
And we worked there, a whole lot of girls.
We used to collect on, on that [GERMAN] and then march to [INAUDIBLE] over the bridge to that factory and work there a day at work and then come home again.
And so on.
And that was not a bad situation.
Coming to think of it, we had a lot of fun.
I mean, between the happiness and the bad rumors, we just started to revive.
And we were lots of girls, young girls, together.
There was [?
Asa ?] and there was the principal, her father.
And there were lots of-- in between work, we used to sit there and gab and--
Gossip.
Funny things.
Just, just funny stuff.
The, the stories what it-- it had a very, there was something evil was going on.
I mean, there was this one guy-- his name was Max Gore-- among the old people.
He was a drunkard.
He smelled.
He was ugly.
He, he was like a portrait by Picasso.
One eye came out here, the other came out-- no, seriously!
They stank for wide heaven.
And he took advantage of the situation and bedded one of the most beautiful girls of us.
Oke ?] was her name.
And she was a widow by then.
By then she was a widow already.
Her mother-in-law was there with her.
And she-- I, I, I, I not quite get it.
I mean, that was her passport to, to life, that [?
Oke. ?] And she did not discourage it at all.
And he used to torment her.
He used to have her wear-- schlep the most heaviest bags of, of, of, of shoes and hit her on the behind.
She was a glorious, gorgeous looking, tall, beautiful girl, [?
Oke. ?] That had a bad [? thing. ?] That, that-- it was very unpleasant.
And I mean, Ribbsdor, Dietmar, and Hanke couldn't have been nicer.
They treated us with respect and consideration.
They ran the, the depot.
Paul von Ribbsdor was the chef.
And after work, we had to march home and sing on the road.
We had to sing something, which was a march.
I mean, we sang.
We sing [INAUDIBLE].
There were wonderful girls among them.
There was one girl called Irena Ginsburg, who was our clown.
We used to say, [?
Irinka, ?] make a funny face!
And then she could do anything with her face.
And she was so funny, and she was there with her mom.
And Kurt Dietmar was madly in love with her.
Madly in love with her.
He did anything to save Ire.
He took her to Germany.
He, he-- they bleached her hair white blonde.
But she wouldn't leave her mom.
She came back.
She always went-- come back to mom, [?
Inka. ?] Well.
There were very often, as the SS came and had [INAUDIBLE] and grabbed as many Jews [INAUDIBLE].
I, I think they had a quota.
They had to deliver so and so many corpses a day, a week, a month.
I don't know how their bookkeeping was kept.
And those people tried to help us and save as much as possible.
Send us home, help us hiding, and, and delivered only the minimum.
They to deliver something.
Then came December.
The 13th, 14th, 15th December.
And we had-- the rumors were about, which didn't sound so good.
Russian prisoners of war have dug trenches outside and put chalk and a little some kind of chemicals there.
What we think was done?
We didn't want to think.
The Latvians-- some Latvians were-- some would tell, warn us, and some where gloating that you're going to get killed.
All of you are going to get killed because they are preparing it.
No, come on.
Give me a break.
I mean, all of us?
Yeah?
And then they had an announcement in, in the, in the newspaper-- in the Jewish newspaper.
There was one Jewish [INAUDIBLE] I think only for the Jews to read.
The Jews are going to get more cottage cheese from now on.
Now come on.
Where is the logic?
If they announce to give us more cottage cheese, are they going to kill us?
For-- of course not.
This is nonsense.
I looked in the mirror.
I didn't change at all.
I mean, they're going to kill us [INAUDIBLE] tomorrow.
I mean, I, I was the same.
And, and then there came an announcement we are not to leave our homes.
With trepidation.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We lived together now because some people were burnt out.
And we shared our apartment.
We all lived with Tante Minna's house.
Me and my mom and the, and, and little Miriam and Tante Minna and Tante Minna's girlfriends, Roska and [?
Silka. ?] Her husband wasn't there.
But [?
Schmiel ?] was there, Roska's husband.
And Roska was pregnant then at that time.
It was her first child.
We all lived in that house together.
But before that, I have to tell you another story.
Before the [INAUDIBLE], I worked at, at the harbor and we were cutting lumber on a machine.
And while I was working, a man was observing me.
And it was a Dane.
Nothing but a Dane.
And he came over and said to me, you know, I have an idea.
Why don't you come with me and we go with a boat over to Sweden?
I came home and told it to my mom and all that.
And then [?
Schmiel ?] said, come on.
I mean, honestly.
We didn't hear of that [INAUDIBLE] story.
I was a good looking girl and it was an old, old, oldish gentleman.
He was married.
Who knows what he's going to do to her and-- OK.
He comes to a certain hour, at a certain hour, to fetch me.
And he came.
And [?
Schmiel ?] said, no, don't go.
And I didn't.
And he was so insulted.
He took-- and now again, he visited a Jewish home, which was very dangerous.
That was my chance of becoming a Dane was gone.
Then those days came, and we were meant to stay home.
And people were picked up and taken somewhere.
We didn't know where.
And I had still a book to finish to read.
Yeah.
There was a French [INAUDIBLE] writer called [?
Adriane ?] [?
Toma. ?] And she wrote wonderful books for young people, which are called, And it all dealt with World War II.
And Catherine was such a hero, she went from [?
Metz ?] to Strausbourg.
She bought-- I don't know whose side she was on.
I forgot already.
But she was kind of-- brought messages.
She was a real hero.
She was my hero, and I had to finish the book.
And that was a day we were supposed to stay in.
And I was reading and reading.
We all had prepared our clothes and everything.
My Tante Minna was safe because she was this marvelous, marvelous seamstress.
And she was doing clothes for the mistress of, of the, of the head of the [INAUDIBLE], of the Gestapo.
The [INAUDIBLE].
So she was safe.
She had an [INAUDIBLE].
She had a pass that she was safe.
Most of other people were-- [INAUDIBLE] wasn't there anymore, my girlfriend.
She worked-- she cleaned houses for the, for the Gestapo too.
But one day, Baumgartner, one of those people said to her, you know, [INAUDIBLE], it's very sorry.
But this afternoon we will have to kill you because I got the orders.
So she wasn't there anymore.
She was killed before.
He had changed the whole group of, of, of Jews then.
Killed the ones, killed the ones which are working and brought in another, another crowd of Jews.
I don't know what the politics were, why they did that.
But anyhow, I was reading my book.
It was kind of late.
And my mom said-- I slept with Miriam in the other room-- and she said, why don't you turn off the light?
I [INAUDIBLE] get a rest!
Now, that I didn't get.
If they kill me tomorrow, I will rest.
If they don't kill me tomorrow, who the hell cares?
I want to know what happened to Catherine!
So I was reading until they knocked at the door and the, the Latvian [INAUDIBLE], he came to get us.
And oh that cold!
Bitter cold.
Cold, cold, icy.
The sidewalks were just covered with ice.
So they came, and they read the names.
And we all had to identify ourselves.
They took our coats, they took our bundles.
Bundles, we thought we need bundles.
And they-- we had to stay downstairs in front of the gate, wait for the other groups from the other houses.
People came out, people came out.
Then I said to the [INAUDIBLE], I have to go up again, because you see, I'm not going anywhere without a lipstick.
[LAUGHS]
I don't know.
I told you [INAUDIBLE] [? '31. ?] It was not that young, but it was silly enough.
So [LAUGHS] so he said, go ahead, go ahead.
And I go up and my Tante Minna opens the door.
Her face was contorted in anguish.
I said, don't worry, Minna.
I'm going to get a lipstick.
[LAUGHS] And I went down there, and they escorted me-- us, all of us down to the prison-- women's prison there where they collected us.
The next door neighbors, there was a little girl.
And I do remember, she said, Papa, I'm afraid.
And he said-- Blume was her name.
And he said to her, don't be afraid, my child.
It's Hanukkah.
The-- a miracle is going to happen.
And we slithered, slithered, and slithered and fell and came.
And then when we came into the-- they opened the door and pushed us into that [? in a ?] cell, all hell broke loose.
The [INAUDIBLE], the Latvians, they're drunk.
They carried on!
They threw [INAUDIBLE].
They yelled.
And, and it was-- oh, people were screaming!
We had to line up.
And my Tante Minna said to Mom, you know, when you get the prison, ask for to call Mrs.
Kroneberg, who was the Latvian mistress of the head of the Gestapo.
She said she's going to help you.
So she went to this office and said, I would like to call Mrs.
Kroneberg.
The girl at the, at the desk said, all right.
Calls, said, she's not in.
Mrs.
Kroneberg is not at her desk.
Would you like to wait?
We will wait.
We stood there and waited.
Can I call now Mrs.
Kroneberg?
Mrs.
Kroneberg is on lunch.
She is not answer the phone.
Hey!
She takes lunch and we're about to be killed?
Then, then at this time, we were standing there, they dragged in my grandpa, who was such a quiet man and never said.
But he wouldn't want to go.
He didn't want to go.
He-- they had to-- two [INAUDIBLE] grabbed him and one pushed.
He was like a-- like an ox!
He just didn't move.
[POINTING] And he foamed at his mouth and his eyes were bulging.
And we were standing there-- his daughters, his grandchildren-- helpless, looking at, at-- it was incredible!
And Mom said, the third time she's calling Mrs.
Kroneberg, she is still out for lunch.
So we had to line up with the others in the yard and waited to be transported wherever they transported us.
We didn't walk.
They transported us in those days, and I remember, on trucks.
I don't know.
There-- we were over-- listen, we were 11,000, 12,000 Jews. 4,000 they killed in July.
The rest they collected, more or less.
Exceptions the one which had passes, which were so important for the war.
Like my Tante Minna who, who, who did clothes for Mrs.
Kroneberg.
And by the way, the German lady's name helped us was Miss Kroneberg.
Oh, we got the same name.
She was a good one, that was a mean one.
All right, Mrs.
Kroneberg had lunch-- the Latvian Mrs.
Kroneberg-- and didn't give a damn about us.
And we had to line up with the others.
And I remember my teacher, Mrs.
Friedlander-- [POINTING] my English teacher Miss Friedlander-- Mrs.
Friedler.
Then she was married to Professor [?
Lang-- ?] was standing in front of me.
And I said to her, do you know what, Mrs.
Friedlander?
When we are going where we are going, let's talk only English.
By that time we're out of there, I will speak English beautifully and go straight to America, have a house with a, with a white picket fence and marry Mickey Rooney, probably.
Possibly.
[LAUGHS] She said, ja, my child.
Yes, my child.
Yes, my child.
She knew.
Yeah.
And then it happened.
In came Paul von Ribbsdor, Kurt Dietmar, Otto Hanke.
Dressed in the lovely uniforms with the, with the beautiful golden stripes, looking fine.
Stood there and said, Saks, [GERMAN]!
I said, [INAUDIBLE] Paul von Ribbsdor, my-- [SPEAKING GERMAN] is here.
And my, my mommy's here and my little-- Don't you worry, he said.
[SPEAKING GERMAN] Anybody he knew by name, grabbed us, marched us out from there, took us on his truck.
And the Latvian [INAUDIBLE] 'cause they didn't like the idea at all.
I mean, here they had somebody to play with.
And we are leaving!
They came running after us!
And he, he bellowed at them-- Paul von Ribbsdor-- [SPEAKING GERMAN] You know.
[SPEAKING GERMAN] You know.
They went back to the yard and tormented some other Jews.
I do remember while we were sit-- I was-- in that, I will never forget [INAUDIBLE].
It was the most, one of the most horrendous memories.
I was sitting on that, on the truck, being 'saved,' quote, unquote.
And I see my Hebrew teacher, Mr.
Finkelsstein, who just had a newborn couple of boy-- boys twins.
They were maybe just nine, eight, nine months old.
In each arm, a little boy chick climbing on the truck being taken to their death.
And I saw him carrying those kids up.
Oh, I can never forget that!
That was my last image when I left.
My, my teacher Finkelsstein, which it was such a big deal.
For years he didn't have babies.
Then he had those twins.
Two little beautiful boys.
Yeah.
And they took us-- and those Germans took us back to the factory where we worked and had us hide in the basement among the blankets.
And there we slept, and there they brought us food.
And every night, Paul von Ribbsdor used to come down and tell us that we shouldn't worry at all because they're going to defend us to the last.
Yeah, but they said that, which was very nice.
And once it was over, it was over.
I mean, that's how it was.
Once the happiness was over and [GERMAN] they called, was over, it was over.
You were safe again.
For a while.
Then it was over.
And we went, we went home again.
And we were-- then we knew who was missing, who wasn't there.
And then the news came in what really happened out there where they take us.
Somebody came back, crawled out.
Always somebody who crawled out from under.
They had to go there.
They had to undress in that cold, horrible thing, put all the clothes in one spot, line up against a trench and were machine gunned down.
Not Germans, Latvians.
Latvians were so clever.
They used to line up one after another and could, with one bullet, shoot two.
As they were even exporting Latvians executioners to all over.
To Lithuania, to [INAUDIBLE].
They were very good at that.
When it got dark, they had them herded in their barrack and to wait till the light and [INAUDIBLE].
And there was this wonderful lady whose name I forget, forgot.
But she had red hair.
And she collected all the little children around her and told them stories about whatever entertained them all night so they shouldn't be frightened.
And I, I-- that is a very, a [BACKROUND NOISE] very touching picture to think of.
And in the morning, they walked them out again and they [INAUDIBLE].
There are pictures, photographs of that.
They're sitting-- do you know those photographs where they're sitting, sitting naked?
Those are people from my-- there is a girl from my class.
She sits like that.
Do you know this photograph?
This is-- One here.
Yeah?
Do you have that?
This is somebody-- Jenny Brun.
She sits like that, naked in the ice.
And then at this point, we were left only 800 Jews in town, mostly women.
And we then had to enter the ghetto.
It was a, a woman's ghetto.
It was on [NON-ENGLISH] Strasse, such a block.
And then of course, she had apartments.
Again we were together with, with [?
Schmuleg ?] and Roska and [?
Silka ?] in, in, in one apartment.
My mom got the job.
She took over the gardens.
She did the vegetable.
I went back-- I worked again in the, in the [INAUDIBLE] then from the ghetto again out there.
And we had our own shop.
We had our own police.
Schmuleg ?] was a policeman.
Jula ?] Goldberg was a policemen.
The few boys, they are the police.
And [INAUDIBLE] were the ghetto alteste and his sidekick.
Life was, was bearable.
Our commandant was called Kersher.
He was from the [INAUDIBLE] side.
800 people.
It was a-- I don't know.
Darling, it was--
We were kind of, in, in, in many people around.
It was one-- [POINTING] a few streets like something or other.
And life, as I was going to say, life was bearable.
Because Kersher was a benevolent Nazi.
If he didn't have to, he didn't kill anybody.
I mean, only if he had to.
Things have happened there.
Little children, the Rubinstein children, were taken away because they had a [INAUDIBLE] and, and the mama, the mother was taken.
The mother said, oh my children, my children!
They said, don't worry.
We'll take your children to the [INAUDIBLE].
Threw them into the truck like footballs.
And I remember, I saw [INAUDIBLE] taking warm.
They dressed them [INAUDIBLE].
Had to bring them, dress them up warmly, and they walked them out to a children [INAUDIBLE].
One of the policeman, [INAUDIBLE] he was, he had a bad reputation already what before.
But he was a-- he stole allegedly, the sugar from the, from the shop.
And then they had their order to look for the thief.
And he was among, you know, all the [INAUDIBLE], and he was among the one who looked for the thief.
And then they found out it's him.
He was taken out and he was executed on his own.
It was always more dramatic when, when one was executed on one's own.
You know, a special execution.
[BACKROUND NOISE] Like before the ghetto, in the beginning, we used to go into hiding.
And they, and the Germans grabbed two girls who were from my school and they had to go about in masks and point them out under pressure and blackmail and [INAUDIBLE].
And then they were executed on their own after they [INAUDIBLE].
Which is always has a special eerie, yucky feeling about it.
Oh.
So he was executed on his own.
And those things have happened.
But we could have managed in the ghetto because we still had our contacts where we could exchange things for food.
How?
My-- especially my mom.
Went to work.
People and Latvians were, were bringing some, some potatoes for, for, for a ring.
Yeah?
Mom was, Mama was, knew how to make nothing from-- potato, potato kugel for, from anything.
I mean, she used to make and do it from-- I don't know what she made it from.
So we were not really very hungry.
In the [INAUDIBLE], the people treated us nicely as they always did.
We even had a party once, I remember.
And the-- and Kersher came to the party.
And somebody called-- was telling jokes.
I still remember the first dirty joke I heard.
That was at that evening.
And people are having-- people were getting 'married,' quote, unquote.
Some people got pregnant.
No, not yet.
And then came this-- then we heard the rumors that the ghetto is going to be closed down, and we are going to be shipped out.
The people who worked from Tzivson, there was David Tzivson, who worked for the [INAUDIBLE] and other few men, they knew the story that we are going someplace which is very, very dangerous.
And it, the ghetto is closing.
Our life is over, more or less.
Yeah.
There was a, a, [POINTS] such a fence.
Yeah.
People did escape.
I don't-- somehow didn't come back.
But I don't quite know who.
But I know people went to a certain address in-- in the city where there was a very nice man who put them up.
Some.
I know that David Tzivson gave, was married to somebody called [?
Friedman-- ?] and-- to Jenny [?
Friedman-- ?] and he, the old mother, they gave-- some people poisoned themself.
They gave poison to the old people.
And suicides was galore.
I remember that a, a girl from my school who was pregnant was going through an, an abortion, but without anesthesia.
Can you imagine an abortion in, in, in in that time?
An abortion, the screaming, the hollering.
The next day, we were marched to the, to the train.
Who wanted you out?
Hmm?
SS [INAUDIBLE].
And, and the SS-- the, the Gestapo.
The, the Hanke, [?
Faustman, ?] Baumgartner, and what all those guys were called.
They were there organizing it.
We took with us something.
I don't remember what.
Clothes.
What?
Clothes, food?
I don't-- clothes probably.
Yeah, clothes.
Oh, I took a book.
[INAUDIBLE] it's going to be boring.
No.
But I think it was Stefan Schweig's Yeah, yeah.
I think Schweig.
Well, anyhow, I-- just in case it gets boring, you know, I should have something to do.
Books were very important to me.
That was a wonderful way of escaping the reality.
[BACKROUND NOISE]
So anyhow, and I had then a-- a boyfriend, so to speak.
His name was Leybl Shalmonis.
He was a very-- he was one of the police guys.
And we walked around at night in the ghetto saying goodbye to friends.
Who knows we're going to see again?
I had a cousin then who was, who was 'married,' quote, unquote and, to somebody then.
And see, we were already 19 or so.
So old, yeah?
Or something, I mean.
My-- Did you have a curfew in the ghetto?
Oh, what do you mean?
In the ghetto?
Not.
There were-- my aunt had this, this fabulous gentleman friend, Professor [INAUDIBLE].
She had a hot going affair.
He used to come every day for dinner to our house.
And the conversation was very interesting.
Rose [INAUDIBLE] --that's me-- has a chance to live.
But Minna, no.
Who has a chance to live?
Who has not a chance to live?
You know, I remember Minna's face when she will not have a-- that was taken.
You know, you got so-- it-- the norm was you won't live.
It was natural to be killed.
Not to be killed was unnatural.
But that so-and-so is not going to live, I mean cannot survive, that was a natural thing.
You accepted that.
Minna will not live, but Rose, she has a chance.
Miriam [INAUDIBLE] will probably not live, but so-and-so has the chance.
They had even a little kindergarten in the ghetto as Sigfried [INAUDIBLE] was organizing, he was singing Hebrew songs, and this sort of thing, or at a school.
I don't remember that.
I know that was some kind of kindergarten.
Miriam [INAUDIBLE], was my little sister, was going.
My mom was doing the, the gardening and the young kids were helping push out the weeds.
Food.
Vegetables.
Vegetables.
Vegetables, most of the time.
Carrots and things.
Not that I remember.
That there were religious services.
There were hardly any men.
We kind of socialized a little, you know?
There were some people who, who we were friends or more friends with the others.
As [INAUDIBLE] morals were a little low.
I mean, it was more the '90s in that respect.
[LAUGHS] I mean, come on.
We are dying tomorrow and this sort of thing.
But when our, our ghetto was closed, that was a big shock to us.
It was-- we, we felt already at home there.
We knew how to manipulate.
We knew how to, to stay alive in the ghetto.
We didn't-- I don't think-- I did not think what will happen next.
If I would have thought, I would have been very frightened.
That was my, my [LAUGHS].
I didn't think!
I read my books!
I didn't think what will happen next.
[INAUDIBLE] I had-- we had-- I, I kind of looked forward to a trip stupidly.
We are going to the, to the train.
We are going on a trip.
But that wasn't funny.
Because we were lots of people in this one little place, in this wagon.
We hardly only could stand.
And we were on the same wagon with, with that lady who had the abortion.
She was bleeding so terrible!
Oh!
She was in-- and we had no water.
And we-- and the train was standing.
It didn't even move!
I mean, from Liepaja to Riga should have done in two hours.
It took-- I don't-- forever, it seemed to me.
And there was no toilet.
There was only this horrible little pail.
And it was hot.
It was October, I think when, when we leave us.
It was a hot time.
It was stinking.
It was horrible.
When we arrived at the, at, at last in Kaiserwald, people can't--
